author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
text:
Adding a switch is a lot harder than adding a LED. a LED is just on and off, where a switch is just waiting to bite you on the backside. Going from low to high with a switch to us is just low then high, but to the Arduino running at say 1MHz sees it as low-high-low-high-low-high-low-high. So you click it once and it thinks you have switched it on and off maybe a 100 times.

The next thought after thinking i'll just use a switch, should be what kind of switch debounce I should use. This is a beginners trap, not knowing what happens when mechanical switching meets microcontrollers.

A simple low pass filter on the input or software debouncing.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: That's a fun introduction and an easy example. Even with this simple example there are some things to consider. First of all switches bounce when closing or opening. When you close the switch the led blinks a few times. Way too fast to notice, but it's there. No problem in this example but if you want to count the number of times the button is pressed it might jump from 0 to 4 in one press. Then there is switch current to consider. A switch needs a minimum current to operate reliably. When you forget about it sooner or later the switch is going to fail. That's not specific to arduino btw. Then there is watchdogs. Every well designed embedded computer uses them. When implemented correctly it will reset the controller when it crashes. Due to immunity issues ( conducted or radiated ) it can happen. When not implemented even you're simple program might stop functioning when under stress like that.

author: @smob0
text:
I am pretty sure  you are planing on using this to switch relays. Sometimes arduinos can have issues with this due to current draw demands, if this happens you should consider getting a few optoisolators to add to your circuit. 

I think if you are using a relay shield, they often come with everything you need and you dont have to worry about it.

author: @kenrowe167
text: Snap! I bought the same starter kit. It's brilliant. And there are plenty of tutorials out there to learn all about Arduino programming for those new to the subject. I, too, copy and paste bits of  software but I like to know how it works.

author: @NorthernKitty
text:
Those starter kits are great for people working with Arduinos for the first time!!  Most come with step-by-step projects that get you into the swing of things right away!!  They usually contain everything you need - many include the Arduino!  I think the biggest intimidation factor for most people is the unfamiliarity of "how do I create programs and how do I get them onto the Arduino?".  The kits will usually guide you directly to the on-line resources you need and will take you through all the basic steps.  You don't have to really know anything, you just follow the instructions!!  Understanding will come as you repeat the process.  It's really just downloading and installing the Arduino IDE software, connecting your Arduino to your PC, and then following the instructions to choose the code you need and load it onto your Arduino.

There's a ton of YouTube content out there on the Arduino.  Do a search on "Arduino Introduction" or "Arduino Basics" or "Learning the Arduino", etc.  My advice is that if you start watching a YouTube video on it and it seems too intimidating, stop watching and look for a different video.  There's a lot of easy-to-understand content, it's just a matter of finding it.
